macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek Hamishi and the Opening Dispute on Hekdesh Ta'us

micro_segments.title: Opening statement of the debate: Beis Shammai vs. Beis Hillel on mistaken consecration
Connection to earlier discussion in Perek Sheini on conditional nezirut from non-grape items
Beis Shammai's reasoning: no release from hekdesh means no release from nezirut
Three concrete examples from the Mishnah: black ox, gold coin, barrel of wine
Beis Shammai's position derived from comparison between beginning and end of hekdesh
Temurah works even when mistaken; therefore hekdesh at inception should also work when mistaken
Beis Hillel's counter: temurah is a specific halakhic rule that cannot be applied to hekdesh's beginning
Gemara's challenge: the timing of temurah takes effect only when condition is met, not before

macro_segments.title: Second Explanation: Rav Pappa's Reinterpretation Model (first of the black oxen)

micro_segments.title: Reinterpretation: not two oxen but multiple oxen; he meant 'the first black one to come out'
This reinterpretation means it is not actually ta'us (mistake) but deliberate specification
Ta'us redefined as ambiguous or non-definitive statement (unclear vs. clear hekdesh)
Gemara's objection: Mishnah says 'first to come out of my house' not 'first black one'
Rav Pappa's response to Rav Ashi: the statement comes across as misleading/ambiguous, hence reinterpretation necessary

macro_segments.title: Beis Shammai's Position on True Hekdesh Ta'us and Evidentiary Cases

micro_segments.title: Question: does Beis Shammai hold that true hekdesh ta'us (real mistake) would work?
Mishnah case: one made neder as nazir, consulted chacham who released him, animal was already designated
Beis Hillel challenges Beis Shammai: you agree hekdesh ta'us that goes out to graze—how is that hekdesh?
Analysis: this is not hekdesh ta'us but rather change of status due to chacham's release

macro_segments.title: Third Explanation: Abaye's Past-Tense Vocalization Model

micro_segments.title: The word sheitzei (future) vs. sheyatza (past): different vocalizations of the same letters
Abaye proposes the Mishnah speaks in past tense: an ox that already came out, not future
Explanation: speaker realized white came out instead of black but intended first animal, not black specifically
Gemara's problem: how to extend this vocalization model to the other two cases (coin and wine)
Solutions: change vocalizations in second and third cases to past tense forms to fit the model

macro_segments.title: Analysis of the Three Cases Through 'Good Eye' and 'Bad Eye' (Ein Yafah vs. Ein Ra'ah) Framework

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's principle: quality of oxen varies; black ox in white herd is inferior but black ox may be good otherwise
First case analysis: black ox (inferior in this context); questions whether Beis Shammai gives bad or good
Second case analysis: gold vs. silver; which is better depends on one's intent
Third case analysis: wine vs. oil; quality preference varies by region (wine better in some places, oil in others)
Rav Chisda's resolution: all three cases can work if we assign appropriate quality values by use (plowing, eating, skin)

macro_segments.title: Beis Shammai's Counter-Proof from Maaser (Tithe) Miscount Case

micro_segments.title: Beis Shammai poses: what if someone miscounted and called the ninth the tenth, tenth the ninth, eleventh the tenth?
Despite the mistake, all three animals become maaser; biblical law sanctifies the tenth without requiring intent accuracy
Beis Shammai's argument: if maaser works with ta'us, so should hekdesh—proving hekdesh ta'us is valid
Beis Hillel's response: the rod (shevet) is the source of kedushah, not the person's speech—this is a gezerat hakatuv
Hillel's follow-up: if he mistakenly placed the rod on the eighth or twelfth, nothing would happen—kedushah doesn't result

macro_segments.title: Application to Nazir Case and Distinction Between True Ta'us and Changed Status

micro_segments.title: Why is the animal designated for nezirut's chattat not consecrated even though hekdesh ta'us occurred?
Answer: this is not hekdesh ta'us; it is a conscious, intentional act later undone by chacham's release
Summary: true hekdesh ta'us (mistake in the premises) differs from deliberate action later reversed

topical_tags.term: First of the black oxen explanation
Ein yafah vs. ein ra'ah (good eye, bad eye)
